Event barn and as-built construction: 5 Birds Farm has been a working farm since 2015, it is certified by the state of Vermont as a Small Farm Operation (exhibit 011) and as such works under the RAPs of the State. This application is for 5 Birds Farm to continue running an Accessory on-farm business (at the same location as the farm (Cite as: 24 V.S.A. § 4412 #11)-With permitted use of barn for year round storage, preparation, processing, and sale of qualifying agricultural products. 1. Grows, sells, and cures garlic through traditional barn hanging methods. 2. Grows and sells fresh cut, dried (barn hanging method), and steam distills lavender for essential oils. 3. Prepares flower essences through sun-light distillation and artisan well water with garlic, hemp, and lavender flowers. 4. Grows, dries (barn hanging method), and processes hemp in traditional small batch, low heat electric oven baking and slow warming in crock pots to infuse a carrier oil. There are no significant emissions from the process. Final products incorporate the full-plant and lavender oil. No smokestacks are necessary. The barn is used year round for the storage of agricultural products and equipment. All products are handmade and made on the farm, from plant/seed, to final product. Seasonal educational, recreational, or social events that feature agricultural practices or qualifying products, or both. Social events may include tours of the farm, farm stays, tastings and meals featuring qualifying products, and classes or exhibits in the preparation, processing, or harvesting of qualifying products. Seasonally, March- October, the barn will be used for social events consistent with Act 143. Historically, barn activities have entailed larger gatherings of up to150 guests, approximately 5 to 7 times per year during March-October. Moving forward we are planning a series of approximately 10 to 12 smaller events tied to our wellness farm business targeting groups of approximately 30 to 50 people. Additionally, we are considering 8 to 10 larger events targeting 100 to150 people. All activities have and will conform to performance standards set by the town of Woodstock and there will be no all-day musical activities.

Reconstruction of the Hinesburg Wastewater Treatment Facility which will be completed in two phases. Phase I will include (a) draining and sludge removal of Lagoon #1, (b) taking Lagoon #1 out of service, (c) constructing a drainage layer and underdrain system with groundwater pumping station within the Lagoon #1 footprint, (d) installing subgrade wick drains in Lagoon #1, and (e) filling Lagoon #1 above wick drains for the purpose of consolidating the compressible soil underneath the footprint of a future infrastructure. Phase II will include (a) upgrades to the existing main pump station, (b) construction of a new headworks, (c) construction of two new sequential batch reactor tanks with pre- and post-equalization tanks, (d) construction of a new filtration and disinfection building with associated equipment, (e) construction of a new sludge storage tank and new in-plant pump station, (f) construction of a new control building, and (g) once the new treatment plant is operational Lagoons #2 and #3 will be drained, cleaned and taken out of service.

The Black River Crossing has been determined to be at risk due to existing pipeline exposure and for potential additional exposure due to live bed scour conditions. There is also evidence of lateral bank migration at the Project site with resultant toe scour and under-cutting along the near-vertical northern bank. The purpose of the pipeline protection project at the Black River Crossing is to provide a stable layer of protective cover within the pipeline working corridor and provide bank stabilization to reduce risk of pipeline exposure and potential future pipeline damage exacerbated by ongoing geomorphic fluvial processes at the project site. Lateral bank migration, live-bed scour, and presence of large woody material within the Black River have increased the risk of pipeline exposure in the river channel, which poses risks to pipeline integrity. There are no buildings or permanent roads proposed as part of this pipeline protection project.

The proposed building program consists of 17 one bedroom apartments, 5 two bedroom apartments and 2 studio apartments. 2 of the one bedroom apartments will be accessible, one of which will also include audio visual accessibility features. 1 two bedroom apartment will be accessible. 

Gross square footage is 21,400 square feet on three floors, 7,000 square feet footprint.

The site will be improved with a net reduction of impervious area, increased and more landscaping, improved parking layout and circulation, sidewalk & walkway improvements, and proposed stormwater management.  Existing chalet lodging unit building to remain. The property will increase from 54 to 76 lodging units. The existing restaurant in the pool building will remain.

Heat from the Ryegate Biomass Power Station will be recovered and used to serve a drying system, which will dry wood chips, the majority of which will then be processed on-site into wood pellets (“Project”).  The Project will be accessed from the existing Rte 5 Power Station entrance.  Road construction within the Project site will be completed to accommodate vehicle flow. The Facility will consist of a scale, truck dumper, moving floor sorters, wet milling line, drying island, intermediate storage, truck loading, two pellet boxes, and a bagging line. Approximately 90,000 tons of wood pellets per year will be produced and sold to customers in Vermont and surrounding states. Dry wood chips may also be sold to off takes such as other pellet mills, small-scale commercial wood heating systems, and other markets. The wood pellet production will require up to 5 employees, and will encompass an approximately 7-acre footprint on land adjacent to the existing Power Station.

Description: This permit specifically authorizes the construction and operation of a nature trail network comprised of approximately 3,000 linear feet of trails, 7 sensory stations at which three mobile kiosks would be stationed, a 64-square-foot trailhead structure converted from an existing residential outbuilding, parking in a preexisting parking area, vehicular access via an existing residential driveway, wastewater service via portable toilets, and a flag sign (the “Project”).  The trails, in existence prior to 1960, will be improved with a 5-foot-wide, 4-inch-deep crushed gravel surface.  The sensory stations will be located alongside the trails, have a pervious surface, require no tree clearing, and will feature natural materials.  The Project is located at 540 Upper Prospect Street in Barre Town, Vermont.

This permit specifically authorizes the construction of three remote huts for commercial use. Construction will occur in phases over three years: with one hut being constructed per year. Construction activities include clearing brush and minor tree cutting with no ground disturbance, constructing huts using on and off-site materials, and minor trail maintenance (the Project). Guest access to the carry-in/carry-out sites will be non-vehicular via existing hiking trails. Guest parking to be at existing parking area on Champ Lane. Platforms and huts will be used for brief over-night stays in a natural setting. The sites will have composting toilets yielding no liquid, no connection to water source, no interior plumbing except small amounts of hauled in water (±10gal/day/hut) and a sink. All contents from composting toilets will be disposed of in compliance with VT WW Rules § 1-929. The Project is located at Champ Lane in Bolton, Vermont.

Description: This permit specifically authorizes the construction of a new sewage forcemain that runs from the Town of Shelburne's Treatment Plant #2 on Turtle Lane, along the Ti Haul Trail to Bay Road, to Treatment Plant #1 on Crown Road. The project will consist of approximately 7,545 linear feet (LF) of 14” HDPE sewer forcemain installed via directional drill, 445 LF of 6” high-density polyethylene (HDPE) sewer forcemain installed via directional drill, and 3390’ of 12” polyvinyl chloride (PVC) sewer forcemain installed via open trenching, 3930’ of 4” PVC sewer forcemain installed via open trenching, approximately 2280’ of 6” PVC sewer forcemain installed via open trenching and cleanout vaults and all other necessary appurtenances (the “Project”). The Project is located at 53 Turtle Lane, 81 Crown Road, and along Bay Road and the Ti Haul Trail, in Shelburne, Vermont.

The Project will improve the preexisting use of Camp Sunrise, previously developed and operated by the Green Mountain Council of the Boy Scouts of America, and more recently by Outright Vermont.  Camp Sunrise was constructed circa 1924 and has not been substantially changed since 1970 and is therefore a preexisting use. 

The proposed improvements will improve safety, accessibility, and the overall educational experience for campers, staff, and other users of Sunrise.  The Project includes: 1) a central multi-use bathhouse, activity space, and emergency shelter (the "Hub Building"); 2) conversion of lean-tos and tent platforms to rustic bunk cabins; 3) additional plumbed bathrooms and showers; 4) a screened dining pavilion as an addition to the existing dining hall; and 5) associated stormwater and access path improvements.  These improvements were designed to cohere with the existing natural setting and historic character of Sunrise and are proposed within the existing development.

Barred Woods LLC proposes the construction of one 6000 sq ft facility in the Town of Belvidere, Vt. Barred Woods owns an 867.5-acre parcel that is currently a maple farm that gathers sap from approximately 32,000 maple trees and processes it into maple syrup in a sugarhouse located on the parcel.  The new building and its associated development will be constructed upon an approximately 1 acre portion of the parcel.  The new building will include a commercial kitchen for producing and packaging food items including maple value add products such as maple sugar and maple granola. Barred Woods intends to use its own syrup produced on the maple farm in the new facility as well as bulk syrup bought from local maple producers. The amount purchased from local producers may exceed 50% of the volume, weight, and sales of syrup from the building. No new roads or driveways are proposed, though the new building will have access to the existing driveway.

Project seeks after the fact permitting for the historic conversion of a former farm prop. to commercial storage. Use for commercial storage ceased in August of 2025. Applicant purchased the property in 2020, and was unaware of permit compliance issues.  The change in use from farm to commercial likely occurred circa 1989, 31 years before the applicant purchased the property. 

Under the applicants ownership no buildings were erected, impervious areas constructed, and no other changes made that would represent a trigger to an ANR permit program.  

The applicant erected no new permanent buildings or shed.  Self contained storage trailers  were placed near the north and east side of the driveway.  Since operations ceased the storage trailers have been removed.

In response to people entering the property unlawfully, dumping trash near the river and setting campfires, the applicant erected a chain link fence with security screening.  Fence was donated and self installed.

Proper recycling of metal at local recycling and waste oil at the local NAPA store, This will be completed with state and local regulations.  The operation will be confined to this building and the parking shown in the diagram.  The rest of the property will not be used for junk or car for storage.

The existing use of the property is for outdoor education classes by the Hartford Area Career and Technology Center and the  Hartford School District.  The proposed project is construction of a 200 LF driveway and 7900 SF parking area to provide safe access to the property for school buses and personal vehicles during classroom site visits to the property.  The parking area will be located in the southeast corner of the existing field.  A 2-foot high x 250' long earthen berm will be constructed along the northern property line, in the field, to create a detention/infiltration area to offset the increase in stormwater runoff from the proposed impervious surfaces.  No changes are proposed to the remainder of the property or its use.
